Test your child for lead

When your child should be tested for lead
If you think your child might be at risk for lead exposure, ask your
child's doctor
for a blood lead test.
A blood test is required at
one and two years old
if your child:
is in a program such as
Medi-Cal, WIC, or Head Start.
Lives in or spends a lot of time in a place built before 1978 that has peeling or chipped paint or that has been recently remodeled.
If these do not apply, ask your child's doctor about their risk of lead exposure. A blood lead test is free for children enrolled in the programs listed above, and health insurance companies will also pay for this test. Community Health Partnership has set up a phone number to assist families with questions regarding healthcare coverage (MediCal, MediCare, etc.) and access to primary care clinics. For more information, contact
(408)-579-6028
.
What to expect at your child's blood lead test
The test may be done at your child's doctor's office or at a laboratory.
There are two methods of collecting blood for lead testing: capillary or venous. Capillary tests typically use blood taken from your child's finger. Venous tests use blood from a vein in your child's arm.
Depending on the result, your child's doctor may order additional tests. Follow-up blood tests must be
venous.
